summary:
The Normandy landings of 6 June 1944, codenamed Operation Overlord, were the largest seaborne invasion in history and opened the long-awaited second front in Western Europe that accelerated Germany's defeat in [[event:112|World War II]]. Under Supreme Allied Commander General Dwight D. Eisenhower, an armada of nearly 7,000 ships, 11,590 aircraft, and approximately 156,000 soldiers from the United States, Britain, Canada, and other Allied nations descended on five designated beaches along a fifty-mile stretch of the Normandy coast in German-occupied France.

The invasion was the product of years of strategic debate and meticulous planning. The Allies used an elaborate deception operation (Operation Bodyguard) to convince Germany that the main landing would come at Pas-de-Calais rather than Normandy, and Hitler's insistence on holding Panzer reserves near Paris rather than committing them immediately to the beachhead proved crucial. Paratroop drops inland preceded the beach landings; the five assault beaches — Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword — saw varying degrees of resistance. At Omaha Beach, American forces faced devastating fire from defenders on bluffs above the shore, suffering approximately 2,000 casualties in the first hours.

Despite the carnage at Omaha and other beaches, the Allies established their beachheads by nightfall and began pouring reinforcements ashore. The construction of Mulberry artificial harbors enabled the landing of enormous quantities of supplies and men. The breakout from the Normandy perimeter in late July 1944 (Operation Cobra) began the liberation of France. Paris was liberated in August, and Allied forces swept eastward toward Germany through the autumn and winter, despite the temporary setback of the German [[event:3338|Battle of the Bulge]] counteroffensive in December 1944.

D-Day is remembered as one of the defining military operations of the twentieth century and a supreme demonstration of Allied industrial power, logistical organization, and multinational military cooperation. The human cost on 6 June alone is estimated at 4,400–9,000 Allied and approximately 4,000–9,000 German casualties, with the true figures uncertain. The liberation of Western Europe enabled the reestablishment of democratic governments across France, the Low Countries, and beyond, shaping the postwar political order in ways that continue to resonate today.
